date: 2026-06-30
video_id: 0NAW99RHdI8
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0NAW99RHdI8
title: Why is China not participating in the AI theme?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42. It's your skipper here, Darius Dale, to present our macro minute for Tuesday, June 30th, 2026. As always, we'll start with the executive summary from today's lead-off morning notes, so let's dive right in. Today's key macro question is, why is China not participating in the AI theme? The short answer is, judging by the all-time low in the MSCI China Index MSCI US Index ratio, investors are assigning a near 0% probability that China wins the {quote} AI race. In our view, this represents a historic mispricing given the physical supply constraints and the growing political backlash to data centers in the US. China's strategic advantages as low-cost open-source AI provider, as a low-cost open-source AI provider, uh with unlimited political capital and a stranglehold on critical mineral supply have been egregiously undervalued, in our view. Uh in terms of analytical nuance, the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100 are on track for their best quarterly gains in 6 years, driven by the advent uh of the fresh bubble in AI stocks in response to the years-long bubble in AI CapEx. The Nasdaq 100 is up 25% in the second quarter. The S&P [clears throat] 500 advanced 14% in the second quarter. That compares to the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index's 81% melt-up in the second quarter, its best quarter ever. SOX is up 94% in 2026, tracking its best year since 1999. The CBOE Semiconductor ETF Volatility Index is up 83% this year, its biggest annual increase ever. Recall that stocks up, vol up is classic bubble behavior, which is precisely what we observed in gold prior to its peak in January. Contrast this with AI stocks, which are faltering in 2026 after a banner AI-driven year. MSCI China Index down after Indonesia. International investors remain content to plow capital primarily into US markets for now, something we can observe via the dramatic underperformance of the Korean won and Taiwan dollar relative to the respective export growth rates of each chip-making economy. This means that instead of converting the bulk of the proceeds into Korean won and Taiwanese dollars, Korean and Taiwanese exporters like Samsung, SK Hynix, and Taiwan Semiconductor are largely reinvesting their windfall profits back into US capital markets. Circular referencing. Circular [snorts] financing notwithstanding, uh {quote} the petrodollar is evolving into the AI dollar right before our very eyes. All told, 44 AI companies are currently projected to contribute about 60% of S&P 500 earnings growth in calendar 2026, current earnings growth roughly 41%, which is double the rest of the index. Uh the dramatic outperformance was unequivocally justified. Will it continue to be justified in linear fashion or will the confluence of crowded positioning and deteriorating macro and micro signals perpetuate a deepening correction? To this point, OpenAI is reportedly weighing an IPO delay, which is a red flag for major AI chip suspender. As always, I'll wrap up with a question from our community. This one's titled hyperscalers. Says anyone thought about why these cash flow kings would continue to spend like crazy, ruin their free cash flow, and then start to borrow, dilute their shareholders for this promise of AI, which many are clearly showing has promised in defined tasks, but nowhere near generative AI, and which appears to be low margin if China continues to push the price lower with their open-source models? Why would they continue to double down? Zuckerberg was caught on an open mic asking Trump that if CapEx if if his CapEx number was high enough. Bill Gates said recently, "If you're going to be a tech company, then you have to be a tech company." Why are they doing this? Did the administration backstop them to continue the CapEx bubble? Convince me otherwise, please. So, obviously, in my opinion, so thoughtful question, but my opinion, I think the answer to this is pretty obvious. Obviously, the administration has backstopped these guys. You know, this is a national security a strategic national security security imperative for the administration. You know, whether you believe it or not, it is certainly what they've stated as a clear objective is quote and quote winning of the AI race. And so, you know, I'll leave you guys with this. If you do not understand the significance of this photo, then we encourage you to study the academic works of Neil Howe, Ray Dalio, and Peter Turchin. This is the wealth pump personified in one photo. These billionaires didn't pay for their candidate to take the Oval Office and their candidates to take overall every level of government both in terms of Congress, the Supreme Court, state governments, etc. They're not paying for the photo op. So, if they're not paying for the photo op, think about what they're paying for. Next question. Wrap it up there. Darius Dale here presenting our macro minute for Tuesday, June 30th, 2026. Best of luck out there. We'll catch back here tomorrow. Cheers.
